item.title: Australian taxpayers also take on risk for major oil and gas projects. Why is that ignored?
item.description:
analysis

Australian taxpayers also take on risk for major oil and gas projects. Why is that ignored?

Gareth Hutchens

Major energy companies are telling Australians that this country's oil and gas industry relies "entirely" on private sector investment.

In a recent social media post, the Australian Energy Producers made this claim:

"You might have heard the claim that Australia should tax its oil and gas industry the same way Norway does. But does that comparison really stack up?


"For starters, the Norwegian government is a major investor and co-owner of its oil and gas industry, whereas Australia’s oil and gas industry relies entirely on private sector investment.

"That means, in Australia, the private sector — not government — wears all of the risk and up-front costs, which can be in the tens of billions for just one project."

In other words, the oil and gas lobby argues that Australian governments don't wear any risk in the development of our oil and gas industry and don't assume any up-front costs for major energy projects.

But do these claims stack up to scrutiny?

An industry built on help from governments

Australian Energy Producers (AEP) is the "peak" industry body representing Australia's oil and gas exploration and production sector.

Its members include Woodside Energy, Santos, Chevron, Shell, Origin, Inpex, BP, Hancock Prospecting, Exxon Mobil, and Halliburton, among others.

Many of those companies have well-oiled lobbying and PR operations that have been honed over decades because they know that "help from the government" is exactly what they need.

Take the North West Shelf project, which is Australia's largest oil and gas project.

In a submission to a federal inquiry into the Petroleum Resource Rent Tax (PRRT) in 2016, Western Australia's government had this to say about the North West Shelf:

"In the 1970s and 1980s the state played a pivotal role in securing the development of the North West Shelf project through agreements, financial assistance and infrastructure provision.

"If the state had not made these investments, the project would likely not have succeeded."

It said the North West Shelf project proceeded in two major stages.

The first stage, "the domestic stage," which got the project up and running, involved the production of natural gas, which the (then) State Electricity Commission of Western Australia (SECWA) promised to purchase.

SECWA was a WA government-owned entity. And it didn't promise to purchase some of the project's early gas output. It agreed to purchase all of it.

Why would it do that? So that the North West Shelf project would have a guaranteed customer in its early years to ensure its economic viability.

As the WA government explained in its submission:

"The domestic phase was underpinned by 20-year 'take or pay' contracts, signed in September 1980, between the Project Joint Venturers and SECWA for the supply of 414 tetrajoules per day of natural gas (commencing in 1985) — the entire gas output from the domestic phase.

"These state agreements were essential in ensuring the financial robustness for the North West Shelf project and were key to securing contracts with Japanese utilities for the export phase."

So, state government support was essential, in the beginning, to get the project into a position where it was capable of exporting Australian gas to Japan.

The WA government also explained, in its submission, that the first phase of the project ran into major problems.

It said, in the early 1980s, that key players involved in the venture realised that the early contractual agreements would have to be modified because they were threatening to cause SECWA's financial collapse, which could stall (and potentially collapse) the North West Shelf project.

So in March 1985, the Commonwealth and WA governments and the joint venturers agreed to "share the pain" that was forecast to be borne by SECWA, to keep the whole project alive.

Why would the federal government agree to do that?

In his book Inside the Hawke-Keating Government: A Cabinet Diary (2014), Gareth Evans has a story to tell about that.

A North West Shelf 'bombshell'

Mr Evans was one of the major figures in the Hawke and Keating governments of the 1980s and 1990s. He was the minister for Resources and Energy from 1984 to 1987.

His diary entry on January 24, 1985 provides an insight into the politicking that surrounded the gas project at the time.

"A North West Shelf bombshell," his entry began.

"At a hastily arranged late-afternoon meeting in my Canberra office with the Woodside management, led by its managing director, Charles Allen, and chairman, together with Kevan Gosper as the Shell shareholder and a BHP executive as another major shareholder, they tell me a very unhappy story."

According to Evans, the executives said they were struggling to secure financing for the project, and they needed to be able to tell a consortium of banks that the SECWA "take or pay" contract for domestic gas purchases would definitely be honoured.

But the WA government, led by premier Brian Burke, was playing a risky political game of brinkmanship, in a bid to get the federal government to tip in financially.

"The immediate Woodside objective is to get us to put pressure on the Burke government to give the necessary assurance, but they acknowledge that some greater role for the Commonwealth — including perhaps picking up the $500 million tab for the West Australian Gas Pipeline — may be required if the whole house of cards is not to collapse," Evans records.

"I indicate that I will raise it with the PM at the first opportunity."

A week later, Evans records that he gets word that the North West Shelf project was in danger of "imminent collapse" and Woodside directors (including Brian Loton from BHP and Kevan Gosper from Shell) were flying to Canberra again to see prime minister Bob Hawke and treasurer Paul Keating.

In another diary entry, he records an episode in which Paul Keating gave Woodside's executives "a fairly withering blast."

"The general theme, which seems to me quite justified, is that they have known about the problem for years, done nothing to resolve it, have been too financially weak to deserve a place in this league, and now — despite all their private enterprise protestations — want the federal government to bail them out so that they can lick bowls of cream into the hereafter," Evans writes of Keating's reprimand.

He records that multiple long days of negotiations were held in February and March between the federal government, WA government, and North West Shelf venture partners, which eventually led to a compromise deal being struck to keep the project above water.

As part of the deal, the federal government agreed to forgo millions of dollars of royalties for a number of years.

Who takes on the risk? Who pays the cost?

Let's return quickly to the WA government's submission to the PRRT inquiry in 2016.

As the submission detailed, the WA government supported the North West Shelf project in many other ways, besides the "take-or-pay" contracts.

It said state support included the construction, by SECWA, of the Dampier to Bunbury gas pipeline, at the cost of roughly $1.1 billion, to enable SECWA to deliver the gas it had promised to purchase to major markets in the south-west of WA.

Altogether, it said, WA's expenditure in support of the North West Shelf project was calculated in 2010 by the Western Australian Treasury to exceed $8 billion (in 2010 net present value terms).

"In addition, the Western Australian government incurred costs relating to town site development, schools, hospitals, community facilities and roads that were either directly associated with the project or arising from the economic and population growth accompanying the project," it said.

"These costs have never been officially aggregated."

What did Australian Energy Producers claim again?

It claimed (on LinkedIn and Facebook) that "Australia's oil and gas industry relies entirely on private sector investment" and that "in Australia, the private sector — not government — wears all of the risk and up-front costs."

I asked the AEP about those statements on Friday.

In a response, the AEP's chief executive Samantha McCulloch provided this clarified statement:

"Unlike Norway and Qatar, Australian governments do not take an equity stake or invest directly in our oil and gas industry. Instead, we rely on the private sector to invest billions of dollars and carry the full commercial risk of projects.

"More than $400 billion has been invested in Australia’s gas industry since 2010 and the sector is the second-biggest corporate taxpayer, paying $21.9 billion in taxes and royalties in 2024–25."

That's a noticeable amendment to the original public claim.

But consider that new line in that new argument that the private sector takes on "the full commercial risk" of projects.

Australian governments help to reduce the commercial risk of major projects by building pipelines and other infrastructure, by signing "take-or-pay" contracts that guarantee long-term demand at certain prices, and so on.

By placing that risk on taxpayers' shoulders, governments take a large amount of risk off the gas industry.

It's why more voters seem to be wondering why Australian governments don't take an ownership stake in these projects.

There's no such thing as a self-made billionaire

At any rate, the North West Shelf project is just one project in Australia. There are many others. When you look into the history of each one, you'll find similar stories.

Which raises a final point.

The AEP originally argued that the private sector in Australia, not governments, wears "all of the risk and up-front costs" of major projects. But that argument relies on a very narrow definition of "risk" and "costs."

What about the cultural costs?

When the North West Shelf site was being developed, between 4,000 to 5,000 ancient rock carvings on the Burrup Peninsula were destroyed, and many others were relocated.

In 2020, Rio Tinto infamously destroyed the 46,000-year-old, culturally significant rock shelters in WA's Pilbara so that it could expand its iron ore mine. 

What about diplomatic risks and costs?

Do we remember that Australian intelligence officials bugged an East Timorese cabinet office in the early 2000s during negotiations over shared access to the Greater Sunrise oil and gas field, which sits between Australia and East Timor, and an oil and gas treaty worth tens of billions of dollars?

Which industries in Australia were going to benefit from that diplomatic behaviour?

What about the climate risk and the environmental costs?

As former Treasury secretary Ken Henry said recently, Australia is among the top three fossil fuel exporters in the world, and the costs of burning those fossil fuels are being worn by everyone through climate change.

There's no such thing as a self-made billionaire. Private fortunes depend on the social structures and conditions that allow such fortunes to be accumulated.

And the financial success of Australia's export industries depends on the same principle.

item.description: In 2018, Boulder Creek sued Wolverine in Kent County Circuit Court, alleging the company hid the environmental risk posed by its waste after PFAS ...
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Meet 'Green Death': the burial practices for activists worried about climate change and ...
item.description:
After Moira Cathleen Delaney was diagnosed with an aggressive form of intestinal cancer, her thoughts eventually turned to her eventual death and what she wanted done with her body. Delaney’s love of gardening, birds and the forest inspired her decision to be transformed into soil — literally — through a process known as natural organic reduction.

When she died in October at age 57, her family sprinkled some of her remains under her favorite backyard tree and gave some remains to her closest friends and relatives in glass jars to keep or plant things with.

“For her, it was a very comforting thought to be able to return to the earth in that kind of way, and to have her final physical act contributing to the life process,” said Marcos Moliné, her son.

Interest in body disposal options that are better for the planet has risen in recent years, according to research commissioned by the National Funeral Directors Association. Researchers and industry experts said people worry about how conventional death practices such as embalming, fire cremation and casket and vault burials affect the climate, environment and people’s health. Others simply want their final resting place to be in their cherished outdoors.

“How we die does lead to a substantial impact on not only the people around us and our communities, but the earth itself,” said Mark Shelvock, a psychotherapist and lecturer at Western University in Canada, who co-wrote a paper on green death practices.

What’s legal and available varies by country, state and province. Here are some common options.

Conventional body disposal practices

Embalming, the process of slowing down a body’s decomposition, involves known carcinogens like formaldehyde that get injected into veins to preserve the body for viewing and services. The Environmental Protection Agency determined the gas poses an “unreasonable risk” to public health, with people who work in settings where it’s used at greatest risk. Research has shown the likelihood of the gas contaminating soil and groundwater is low.

Fire cremation is the most popular option in the U.S., preferred by nearly two-thirds of the respondents to the funeral directors association. The energy for burning is often derived from fossil fuels. The Cremation Association of North America estimates the energy used in a typical cremation is equivalent to powering a 2,000-square-foot (186-square-meter) home for a week.

Materials used for caskets and vaults are often made of wood, metal or concrete that require mining or tree cutting and huge amounts of energy to produce. Concrete, for instance, is responsible for roughly 8% of global carbon dioxide emissions and 2% in the U.S., most of which come from producing and processing the cement to make it.

Cemeteries also take up space and require maintenance, which involves energy and resources for mowing, watering or fertilizing.

“There’s all of this management that goes into conventional cemeteries that looks very much like a golf course,” said Samuel Perry, a funeral director and president of the Green Burial Council, a global nonprofit that guides green burial standards and certification.

Natural burials

At Prairie Creek Conservation Cemetery in Florida, natural burials are combined with land conservation. Graves are dug by hand and bodies are only buried in caskets or shrouds made of biodegradable materials like bamboo or cotton. Embalmed bodies and vaults aren’t allowed, and to bury cremated remains, they must be in biodegradable, chemical-free urns.

The idea is to let the body decompose naturally.

It’s an idea Scott King was skeptical of when his mother, Linda, said she wanted to be buried this way. But the more he researched, the more he understood the beauty of its simplicity. She was buried in a meadow at Prairie Creek cemetery in October last year, and recently, King buried his brother Kenneth there, too, close to her.

“Through death, life begets life,” said King. “She really liked that idea, too, that she can, in her passing, help give life to something else. That was very important to her. My brother, I think, sort of went along with the ride.”

The cemetery partners with a land trust to manage, restore and protect the land from development. They remove invasive species and plant native ones, and perform prescribed burns.

The benefits, said executive director Heather Grove, reach further. There’s more biodiversity and wildlife now, and “if you want to talk about carbon capturing and all that, conservation is key to sequester,” she said.

According to the Green Burial Council, a green burial sequesters about 25 pounds (11.34 kilograms) of carbon.

Elena Slominski, a researcher who’s studied eco-friendly disposal options, said a conservation burial “is by far the best thing you can do because it’s actually, technically a carbon sink. It actually restores ecological habitat and protects the land.”

But it’s not a good option where space is at a premium, and some argue it’s not practical because it takes up space.

Natural organic reduction — turning bodies into soil

Earth Funeral is a company specializing in natural organic reduction, also known as terramation and human composting. The body remains in a sealed vessel for 30 to 45 days with mulch, wood chips and flowers. Inside, microorganisms break down the body into soil in a natural process that generates heat to reach temperatures of 131 F (55 C) or higher — enough to kill germs.

“What we are fundamentally doing is using science and technology to accelerate a completely natural process” using renewable energy sources, said Tom Harries, the company’s co-founder and CEO. Some of the soil is given to loved ones, and the rest is donated to conservation or reforestation projects.

Fourteen U.S. states allow such a process, and 15 others have introduced bills to make it legal, according to Earth Funeral. Experts said it’s a good option if you live in a city where burial space could be limited and more expensive.

Alkaline hydrolysis — using water for cremation

Alkaline hydrolysis is also supposed to mimic and speed up natural decomposition. At the Colorado-based water cremation company Be a Tree, bodies are put into a vessel with 95% water and 5% potassium hydroxide that is then heated to about 200 F (93 C) for about 18 hours.

Skeletal remains are air-dried, processed and returned to loved ones as powder in an urn or shaped as stones. Most families keep some of the liquid for houseplants or gardening, but most of it goes to land conservation partners to be used as fertilizer, said founder and CEO Emily Nelson. Other companies discharge the residual liquid with other wastewater.

This process uses about 90% less energy than fire cremation.

Perry, with the Green Burial Council, said he’s often asked whether the way a person chooses to be disposed of after death makes much of a difference in their environmental footprint.

“The quick and dirty answer is no, I don’t think this one thing is going to change a whole lot. But it’s always about changing industries. And as an industry, if we do better, we are making a bigger impact.”

___

Daniel Kozin contributed to this report from Gainesville, Fla.

___

The Associated Press receives support from the Walton Family Foundation for coverage of water and environmental policy. The AP is solely responsible for all content. For all of AP’s environmental coverage, visit https://apnews.com/hub/climate-and-environment

Apply here.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Only two BND candidates respond to environmental group's questionnaire
item.description:
BROWNSVILLE, Texas - The South Texas Environmental Justice Network sent questionnaires to all six candidates running for the Brownsville Navigation District board. Of the six, only two candidates responded.

Shariff Gonnella, a candidate for Place 2, and Prisci Roca Tipton, a candidate for Place 4, were the ones that answered the questions.

David A. Garcia and Alex Najera, candidates for Place 2, and Luis Villarreal and Martha Davila, candidates for Place 4, did not respond at all.

“The development of projects at the Port of Brownsville, such as the buildout of Rio Grande LNG, Texas LNG, SpaceX, the recently announced America First Oil Refinery, and Saronic Technologies, raises urgent questions about our community’s ethics, water security, public health, and long-term resilience,” said Bekah Hinojosa, co-founder of STEJN.

“The decisions made by Port Commissioners are felt for so much longer than a four-year term; they’re felt for generations. These decisions shape our landscape and impact our water, air, and health.

“We were looking forward to learning how the Port candidates plan to address these ethical and environmental concerns. And so, we're incredibly disappointed that four of these candidates gave us no response to our environmental candidate questionnaire.”

Gonnella is president of Brownsville & Rio Grande International Railway, which runs the OmniTrax rail line inside the Port of Brownsville. He provided depth in his answers.

Roca Tipton, a higher education administrator, provided shorter answers than Gonnella.

Here are some examples:

What specific measures will you advocate for to ensure that there is full transparency in the Port of Brownsville’s operations, such as responding to public records requests and public access to site lease agreements and negotiations with corporations?

I understand the Port should operate under public information laws and can do better in how we communicate. People shouldn’t have to dig for basic information. Clear summaries and timely updates can build trust without slowing down business.

Transparency and accountability are fundamental to building public trust. As Port Commissioner, I would support timely and complete responses to public records requests, clear communication with the public, and greater visibility into major agreements and decisions, while still respecting legal and competitive requirements.

I would also advocate for quarterly town halls where the Port can provide updates on its priorities, major projects, and ongoing work, while giving the public a direct opportunity to ask questions and provide feedback. In addition, I would encourage more community members to attend the twice monthly Board meetings so they can hear discussions firsthand, stay informed on projects, and have a voice in the public process.

I also believe that before any significant vote on a project that may carry serious environmental, public health, or quality of life impacts, the public should have a meaningful opportunity to be informed, ask questions, and be heard.

The community should be informed, engaged, and able to understand how and why decisions are made, especially when those decisions may have lasting impacts on Brownsville and the surrounding region.

Given the increasing industrial water use demand for the current industries at the Port, what steps would you take as part of the Port Commission to ensure sustainable water management that prioritizes water availability for families to meet their basic needs?

We’re in a real water situation right now, and that has to be taken seriously. I would want to see long-term planning that includes both residential needs and industrial growth, working closely with our water authorities so we don’t overextend our supply.

Water security is one of the most urgent issues facing our region, and it must be addressed with discipline and long-term thinking. As Port Commissioner, I would support requiring a clearer review of industrial water demand before major projects move forward, stronger expectations for water efficiency and reuse, and closer coordination with local and regional water authorities to make sure growth at the Port does not outpace the region’s actual water supply. The needs of families and the community must always come first.

I also believe we should encourage innovation. There are examples around the world of facilities that significantly reduce or offset their water and energy consumption through rainwater capture, recycling systems, and integrated design. While not every solution applies everywhere, we should be looking at how to incorporate those principles into future

development at the Port. The goal is simple: responsible growth that does not outpace our resources and does not place additional strain on an already limited water supply.

Large, multi-billion-dollar corporations are proposed for the Port of Brownsville, including LNG export terminals, Saronic Technologies, America First Refinery, a lithium refinery, and the proposed expansion of SpaceX. What is your stance on these proposed refineries, fossil fuel industries, military manufacturing, and private space corporation projects at the Port of Brownsville? Please elaborate.

There are major projects being proposed, and I understand the importance of jobs and economic growth. At the same time, those decisions carry long-term impacts. Each project should be looked at carefully—what it brings, what it requires, and how it affects our community over time.

This is a complex question, but I do not believe all projects should be treated the same. Each must be evaluated on its own merits, and it is important to distinguish between projects that are already established or well underway and those that are still under review.

For projects that are already operating or significantly advanced, such as SpaceX and LNG developments, the focus should be on working with these entities to ensure strong oversight, strict compliance with environmental standards, and ongoing accountability so they operate responsibly and minimize impacts on our community. This also includes making sure there are clear safeguards in place to prevent, monitor, and address any environmental harm before it becomes too late.

For projects that are still under consideration, including proposals such as refineries, lithium processing, or new manufacturing operations, there is a responsibility to evaluate them carefully before any decision is made. That evaluation must include environmental and public health impacts, water and energy demands, long-term economic value, and alignment with the community’s priorities.

My approach is to support responsible development that brings real economic benefit (jobs, investment, and long-term growth) while protecting our environment and quality of life. I will not support projects where the long-term risks clearly outweigh the long-term benefits to our community.

Projects of that scale, including refineries, require a higher level of scrutiny because of their long term economic, environmental, and infrastructure impact. While I understand why people see them as opportunities for jobs and economic growth, it is also important to recognize that they come with real tradeoffs. In many cases, these types of facilities are located away from dense population centers due to emissions, noise, and heavy industrial activity. That highlights the level of impact they can have and why they must be evaluated carefully. Any decision must be based on a full understanding of those risks, supported by data, and weighed against the long-term interests of the community.

I also believe that decisions of this scale must involve transparency and meaningful public engagement, ensuring that the community has the opportunity to be informed, ask questions, and be heard before any final decision is made. The goal is not simply to bring more industry, but to bring the right industry, development that is sustainable, clean, responsible, and aligned with the future we want for Brownsville.

Climate change is an existential threat to us all, but it is primarily low-income communities like the Rio Grande Valley that are forced to deal with extreme heat, hurricanes, flooding, and

extreme drought. What steps would you take as Port Commissioner to address climate change?

We are definitely already seeing the effects—heat, storms, and water issues. I think the Port has to plan for that so we’re not reacting later.

Climate change is already impacting our region, and it must be taken seriously as part of long-term planning for the Port and our community. One of the challenges today is that we do not have a clear long-term master plan that guides how the Port should grow over the next 20 to 30 years. Without that framework, it becomes much more difficult to plan responsibly for issues like extreme weather, water availability, infrastructure resilience, and long-term environmental impact.

As Port Commissioner, I would support the development of a comprehensive long-term master plan that incorporates climate resilience, sustainable growth, and infrastructure planning. That includes preparing for flooding, heat, and storms, improving operational efficiency, and encouraging development that aligns with environmental responsibility.

It also means making sure that major projects are evaluated not only for their economic benefits, but for their long-term environmental impact and how they fit into the region’s future. Planning for growth without considering climate risks is not responsible planning.

My focus is on ensuring that the Port remains competitive while also being prepared for the challenges ahead, protecting our community, our environment, and the long-term sustainability of the region. Addressing climate change at the Port is not about ideology. It is about managing risk, planning responsibly, and making decisions that protect both our economy and our quality of life for future generations.

Retiring BND Commissioner John Wood has endorsed Gonnella. He explained why:

“I have had the privilege of serving as a Port of Brownsville Commissioner since 2014, working alongside my fellow Commissioners, Port leadership, community leaders, and local stakeholders to support responsible growth and strengthen the Port’s role as a vital economic engine for our region.

“As the Port continues to build on its momentum and seize new opportunities, it’s important that we maintain a clear focus on thoughtful planning, effective coordination, and strong stewardship of public resources. That’s why I am proud to endorse Shariff Gonnella for Brownsville Navigation District Commissioner, Place 2.”

Wood said Gonnella has experience in transportation, logistics, and infrastructure. He said he also has practical understanding of how the Port ecosystem operates and what it takes to deliver outcomes.

“Shariff brings deep experience and a collaborative approach grounded in accountability and public trust. I believe he will be a strong addition to the Board and a constructive partner to Port leadership, tenants, and the community as we continue moving the Port of Brownsville forward.”

Wood added: “Shariff is committed to responsible growth, steady leadership, and delivering long-term opportunity for the people of Brownsville.”

In an interview with the Rio Grande Guardian, South Texas Environmental Justice Network’s Hinojosa said her group is very concerned about the level of transparency shown by BND.

“They don't live stream their meetings. They also don't put out any documents about their agenda items before they vote. Cameron County does that, the City of Brownsville Commission, they put the supporting documents with the agenda items for the public for the public to see. We've asked Brownsville Navigation District to do this, and they have ignored that request,” Hinojosa said.

“I’ve also put in numerous records requests. For example, I put in a records request about Project Hercules. What is it? They haven't responded to my records request within the 10 days as they're supposed to.”
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: How language barriers can put lives at risk during severe weather | Illinois Public Media
item.description:
How language barriers can put lives at risk during severe weather

Weather and climate information is primarily reported in English. But, over 41 million Americans speak Spanish at home.

When severe weather warnings are misunderstood or misinterpreted, the consequences can be serious. Language barriers can put non-English speaking communities at risk during emergencies.

University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign Department of Climate, Meteorology & Atmospheric Sciences professors Stephen Nesbitt and Assistant Professor at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign Department of Climate, Meteorology, and Atmospheric Sciences, Joseph Trujillo Falcon, talks to us about the importance of multilingual weather forecasting.

Funding for Weather Realness is partially provided by the Backlund Charitable Trust. If you have a question for a local scientist on this program, please leave a voicemail at 217.333.2141 or email weatherrealness@illinois.edu
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

We attempted to send a notification to your email address but we were unable to verify that you provided a valid email address. Please visit my profile to update your email address if you wish to receive notifications. Otherwise, disable notifications and hide this message.

Your session was unable to be renewed and will be expiring in 0 seconds. Click here to attempt to renew your session.

Whittling down a $7.4M New London school budget gap to $500K
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: 'A sense of dread': Europe's first climate migrants live in constant fear of extreme weather
item.description:
“Everyone has gone mad after the storm. It’s a form of collective PTSD,” says one Greek villager faced with relocation.

If there is one thing etched in Vaios Giatropoulos’s memory from the worst night of his life, it is the helplessness in his son’s eyes. “Now what, dad? We’ve lost everything,” he had asked him tearfully.

They were standing on the roof of their semi-truck for nine hours as floodwaters rose dangerously close to their feet before they were rescued. Storm Daniel, which struck central Greece in September 2023, devastated their hometown of Palamas, leaving behind a chaotic mix of mud, debris, dead cattle, and shattered lives.

Eventually, Giatropoulos moved with his family to a village where their home is on higher ground. Returning is unthinkable. “I don’t want to feel that sense of dread with every drop of rain. For several months, we feared it would flood again. I even thought about seeing a psychologist,” he recalls.

However, he considers his family fortunate because they found a new home nearby quickly. Many Palamas residents, he says, were forced to relocate to nearby cities, to Athens, or even abroad in search of a better life and drier land.

Hundreds of thousands of Europeans have been displaced by extreme weather

Giotopoulos belongs to a steadily expanding group of Europeans: the continent’s first climate migrants. Displacement within national borders is no longer an academic issue, as more people are affected by storms, floods, wildfires and droughts.

The Geneva-based NGO Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC) estimates that approximately 413,000 people were displaced in the EU between 2008 and 2023. So far, 2023 has been the worst year on record, with over 200,000 Europeans internally displaced, mostly due to wildfires and storms.

However, for those affected, the experience varies greatly from one country to another.

Germany faces devastating wildfires and floods

In Germany, the type of extreme weather people are most worried about depends on the region.

In the northeast, an increasing tendency toward drought has made wildfires more common and severe. Last year was Germany's worst wildfire year on record in terms of hectares burned. While most people in this region need not fear a wildfire coming for their home, some farmers have already begun to feel the effects of diminishing rainfall.

Meanwhile, in much of southern and western Germany, the most immediate danger comes not from too little water but from too much at once.

The IDMC recorded 84,000 internal displacements in Germany from 2008 to 2024. The vast majority of these (78,000) were caused by flooding.

The devastating Ahr Valley floods in 2021 represent one of Germany's most impactful natural disasters in recent history, killing 134 people and affecting roughly 40,000 more.

Martin von Langenthal, who was involved in the disaster response as deputy head of the EU Civil Protection and Resource Management Unit for the Red Cross, estimates that 3,500 people were at least temporarily displaced by that event, with homes requiring serious repair or rebuilding.

Beyond the immediate physical destruction, survivors faced long-term resource shortages, living with damaged infrastructure including washed out bridges and wastewater facilities, and a severe lack of essential medical care, doctors and prescription drugs.

“There were a lot of people who could live in their houses again pretty soon, but there was a large group who couldn't live in their houses anymore because they were completely gone or too damaged to enter,” Langenthal says. “And for those people, container homes and sort-of refugee homes were created initially for short-term housing, but the containers were still being used more than two years later.”

A flood-stricken Greek village torn in two

Due to its Mediterranean location, Greece is particularly vulnerable to climate-related disasters. The IDMC estimates that nearly 300,000 Greeks have been displaced since 2008, most due to wildfires, storms and floods.

An example is the village of Vlochos in Thessaly, central Greece. Situated at the lowest point in the region, the village has long been prone to flooding. Older residents still remember with trepidation the floods of 1953 and 1994 when parts of the village were ruined.

But when Storm Daniel struck in 2023, the scale of the destruction was unprecedented. In many homes, water levels rose as high as two metres, leaving them uninhabitable with all furniture destroyed.

In the first weeks following the storm, a sense of togetherness prevailed. Younger residents helped older ones, and families shared whatever resources they had, recalls Vassilis Kalogiannis, the village’s president, with a bittersweet mix of pride and melancholy.

Yet that unity soon eroded, as the scale of the disaster made it clear that relocating the community to higher ground might be the best way to prevent future disasters. The government has offered such an option, provided it is accepted by a clear majority of the village’s residents.

'Where should we go? This is our home'

In the nearby village of Metamorphosis, around 95 per cent of households voted for relocation in a referendum, partly because the village had recently flooded several times.

“Most people are eager to go, they saw the water reaching their roofs. When there’s rainfall many people leave even if there is no real danger,” says Petros Kontogiannis, president of Metamorphosis.

Things are different just a few kilometres away in Vlochos where people are divided – a harsh reality that many communities across Europe are likely to face as climate change wreaks havoc.

The debate has become hostile, sometimes escalating into serious arguments and even physical altercations. “Everyone has gone mad after the storm. It’s a form of collective PTSD,” says a village resident who wishes to remain anonymous.

Many residents have moved to other villages or cities, yet still call Vlochos home and want the village to move to a more flood-resistant location. “Our village is in the wrong location. It’s at the lowest spot in Thessaly, so since 1953 it has been repeatedly at risk,” says Panagiotis Panagiotopolos, a local who now lives in a nearby town. “The decision for Metamorphosis is to relocate although it’s two metres higher than Vlochos,” he adds.

Not everyone is convinced relocation is the answer, however. “Where should we go? This is our home,” says Apostolos Markis, a former police officer who wants the village to remain where it is.

Northern France under water

Vincent Maquignon, 54, will never see his mother’s face again. The last photos he had of her were swept away by torrents of mud that flooded his home. On 2 January 2024, the father of two boys had only a few hours to salvage what he could from his home, including administrative documents and a few personal belongings hurriedly taken with him.

He had lived for 23 years in this house in Blendecques, northern France. “This house embodied our family’s journey: first steps, first tears,” he painfully recalls. “We had to leave everything behind in an eye-blink. There was 1.4 metres of water on the ground floor.”

It wasn’t the first time his town had flooded. Yet every year, conditions worsened. Its classification as a high-risk flood zone made selling his house nearly impossible. “We were trapped,” he recalls.

That wintry day marked the departure of nearly 800 people from Blendecques.

“We are France’s first climate-displaced people. My family and I moved to a house on higher ground in a neighbouring town. It took us over a year to feel safe again, but as soon as it rains, people get anxious,” Vincent explains.

While northern France is facing increasingly severe flooding, the South is burning, the coastline is eroding and Alpine glaciers are melting. Across the country, most municipalities face at least one major natural risk.

Nearly a quarter of France’s population say they would consider moving because of climate risks in their municipality, according to a recent Odoxa survey. In 2022, around 45,000 people were displaced due to environmental disasters, making France one of the countries most affected by climate change in Europe. The focus is now shifting from whether people will move to the scale of displacement.

A continent on the move

If some of the worst-case scenarios outlined by climate scientists materialise, by 2050 Europe is expected to be 2.5°C warmer than pre-industrial levels, meaning that the South will face longer droughts and more frequent extreme heatwaves, while central and western Europe will experience heavier rainfall and higher risk of flooding.

Like Giatropoulos in Greece and Maquignon in France, millions will have to move within their countries in search of employment and better access to public services, or simply a safer environment to live in.

“In the coming years, we will have more and more internal climate migrants,” says Pavlos Baltas, a demographer at Greece’s National Centre for Social Research. “If people cannot live in one place, they will move.”

Go to accessibility shortcuts

Comments
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Climate Risk and Climate Investing - Free EU Sustainability Courses | BoostMySkills
item.description:
Climate Risk and Climate Investing

Context and overview

Responsible Investors are increasingly paying more attention not only to the transition risk but also to physical risk, the financial losses that come from climate change (droughts, floods, storms, etc.), not from the adaptation of the economy to prevent these losses. These concerns go beyond looking for investment opportunities that meet environmental, social and governance (ESG) objectives, while enhancing the value of investing performance. In today’s business environment, it is, therefore, no longer sufficient for organisations to simply acknowledge global sustainability challenges like climate change, resource depletion, and inequality – they’re expected to lead the way through them. The Sustainable Finance MCs series will support the development of knowledge and skills around financial strategies that create value for society and invest in a sustainable future.
The MC “Introduction to Sustainable Finance – Part 4” focuses on Climate Risk and Climate Investing. The MC examines the motivations behind Climate investing, by examining climate risks transmission channels to financial stability, and the challenges involved in integrating climate risk into existing investment processes. It is designed for practitioners or aspiring professionals across the financial services sector looking to improve their understanding of ESG issues. This course builds on an understanding of the fundamentals of investment management and offers the tools and insights needed to develop financial strategies that create value for society and invest in a sustainable future.

Learning objectives

On the completion of the micro-credential, participants will be able to:

Background

Completion of Introduction to Sustainable Finance – Parts 1, 2 and 3
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado
